---

# Proposal: Augmented Primary Literature Analysis — Computational Biology Extension
**BIO316 — Interpreting Primary Research in Molecular & Cellular Biology**

---

**Submitted by:** Bonus Man
**Course:** BIO316 — Interpreting Primary Research in Molecular & Cellular Biology
**Assignment:** Primary Literature Analysis & Experimental Design
**Professor:** Dr. Wimble
**Date:** April 27, 2026

---

## Section 1: Summary

I am proposing to complete the Primary Literature Analysis & Experimental Design assignment in an augmented format that meets all original requirements while adding a computational biology component to Part 4. Specifically, I will strategically select an article that involves computational or genomic methods (such as RNA-seq, proteomics, or single-cell sequencing) and extend my proposed follow-up experiment to include a **Computational Analysis Plan** — a subsection describing the bioinformatics pipeline or statistical modeling approach that would be used to process and interpret the experimental data. This augmentation fits naturally within the existing Part 4 structure under "Methods and techniques" and does not alter, replace, or reduce any of the original requirements. My motivation is to connect this coursework to my career interests in biotech and computational biology, where employers increasingly seek candidates who can bridge wet lab science and data analysis.

---

## Section 2: Original Requirements Met

Every original rubric criterion is preserved in full in the augmented version.

| Original Rubric Criterion | Weight | How the Augmented Version Addresses It |
|---|---|---|
| **Article Selection** — peer-reviewed article from specified journals, published within last 5 years, contains original data, PDF submitted | 10% | Met in full. The article is still drawn from the approved journal list (*Cell, Nature, Science, Journal of Cell Biology, Molecular Cell, PNAS*), published within 5 years, and must contain original data. The only addition is that I will strategically favor articles that also involve computational methods — all original constraints are preserved. |
| **Article Summary** — biological question, background context, experimental approach, key findings, conclusions (1–2 pages) | 30% | Met in full. All five sub-components are addressed in the written summary. The computational approach used in the article will be identified under the "Experimental approach" sub-component (consistent with the requirement to "identify the major techniques used"), requiring no format change. |
| **Data Interpretation** — two figures analyzed for experimental setup, variables, controls, data interpretation, and evaluation of authors' conclusions | 30% | Met in full and unchanged. Both required figures are analyzed with all specified elements. The augmentation does not modify Part 3 in any way; the depth of engagement with the data is maintained entirely. |
| **Proposed Follow-Up Experiment** — rationale, testable hypothesis, experimental design (methods, controls, sample size, variables), predicted results, interpretation of outcomes, limitations | 30% | Met in full. All six required sub-components are present. The augmented version adds a **Computational Analysis Plan** subsection within "Methods and techniques" — describing how the data generated by the experiment would be processed computationally. This is additive; it does not replace or weaken any of the six required elements. |

---

## Section 3: Augmentation Components

**Computational Analysis Plan (addition to Part 4 — Experimental Design)**

*What it is:* A dedicated subsection within Part 4's experimental design that describes how the data generated by my proposed follow-up experiment would be analyzed computationally. This includes: (a) identifying the appropriate computational tools or software pipeline (e.g., DESeq2 for differential gene expression, Seurat for single-cell RNA-seq, or relevant statistical modeling tools in Python/R); (b) describing the flow of data from raw output to interpretable results; and (c) noting any quality-control or preprocessing steps the pipeline requires.

*Why it is career-relevant:* Modern biology experiments — particularly in genomics, proteomics, and imaging — produce large datasets that cannot be interpreted without computational analysis. Biotech employers such as IDEXX Laboratories and the Jackson Laboratory explicitly list data pipeline literacy and experiment design as required competencies for research and data science roles. A biology graduate who can both design an experiment and describe how its data would be analyzed computationally is significantly more competitive in these roles.

*What the deliverable looks like:* A 200–350 word subsection within Part 4, formatted consistently with the rest of the experimental design section. No separate document is required. The section names the specific tools relevant to the experimental method chosen, justifies why those tools are appropriate, and describes the pipeline at a conceptual level (not as code or implementation).

---

## Section 4: Deliverables

1. **Selected article PDF** — peer-reviewed article from an approved journal, within 5 years, containing original data *(original requirement)*
2. **Written assignment** — Parts 1 through 4 as specified, submitted as a single document *(original requirement)*
   - Part 1: Article selection justification
   - Part 2: Article summary (1–2 pages)
   - Part 3: Analysis of two figures
   - Part 4: Proposed follow-up experiment including the **Computational Analysis Plan** subsection *(augmented)*

All materials submitted together by the deadline of **May 23, 2026**.

---

## Section 5: Timeline

| Date | Milestone | Component |
|---|---|---|
| Apr 27 – May 2 | Identify and select article; confirm it meets all constraints and involves computational methods | Part 1 |
| May 2 – May 7 | Read article thoroughly; annotate for summary and figure selection | Preparation |
| May 7 – May 10 | Draft Part 2 (Article Summary) | Part 2 |
| May 10 – May 14 | Draft Part 3 (Data Interpretation — both figures) | Part 3 |
| May 14 – May 18 | Draft Part 4 experimental design (all six required sub-components) | Part 4 |
| May 18 – May 20 | Research and write Computational Analysis Plan subsection | Part 4 — Augmentation |
| May 20 – May 22 | Review, proofread, and finalize full submission | All |
| **May 23** | **Submit** | **All** |

---

## Section 6: Career Rationale

My career interests lie at the intersection of biology and technology, particularly in biotech research and computational biology roles. Industry employers in this space — including IDEXX Laboratories, the Jackson Laboratory, and similar life sciences organizations — consistently list experiment design and computational data analysis literacy as required competencies at the entry level. While this assignment builds strong experimental design skills (which map directly to how data scientists design controlled studies), it does not currently ask students to engage with the computational layer that follows experimental data collection. That gap is significant for students pursuing careers where sequencing outputs, imaging data, or large-scale assay results must be processed through analytical pipelines before any biological conclusion can be drawn.

The Computational Analysis Plan component directly addresses this by requiring me to think through the full arc of a research project: from hypothesis through experimental design through data processing and interpretation. This is the workflow that biotech research associates and computational biology roles perform daily. Completing this as part of my coursework gives me a concrete, defensible example I can discuss with future employers — not just that I can design an experiment, but that I understand what happens to the data it generates.

I am committed to meeting all original requirements of this assignment and am proposing this addition only as supplementary depth, pending your approval.

---

- **Section or course number** — do you have a section number to add to the header?
- **Tone** — does this feel like your voice, or would you like anything adjusted?
- **Computational tool familiarity** — the plan asks you to name relevant tools in Part 4. If you're not sure which tools apply to your chosen article, I can help you identify the right ones once you pick your paper.

Otherwise, this is ready to go.
